1	package runner
2	
3	import (
4		"fmt"
5		"io"
6		"strings"
7	
8		"github.com/an-lee/gh-sr/internal/config"
9		"github.com/an-lee/gh-sr/internal/host"
10	)
11	
12	// validateAgenticPrereqs returns an error if any hard prerequisite for agentic
13	// workflows is not met. This is called by setupNative before any host modifications.
14	func (m *Manager) validateAgenticPrereqs(h *host.Host, rc config.RunnerConfig) error {
15		if h.OS != "linux" {
16			return fmt.Errorf("agentic profile is only supported on Linux")
17		}
18		// Docker CLI check
19		out, err := h.Run(`docker --version 2>/dev/null`)
20		if err != nil || !strings.Contains(out, "Docker version") {
21			return fmt.Errorf("docker CLI not found on PATH")
22		}
23		// Docker daemon check
24		out, err = h.Run(`docker info 2>/dev/null`)
25		if err != nil {
26			return fmt.Errorf("docker daemon not running")
27		}
28		// RUNNER_TEMP check
29		out, err = h.Run(`echo "${RUNNER_TEMP:-}"`)
30		if err != nil {
31			return fmt.Errorf("could not read RUNNER_TEMP env var")
32		}
33		rt := strings.TrimSpace(out)
34		if rt == "" {
35			return fmt.Errorf("RUNNER_TEMP is not set; gh-aw requires it to be set to a path other than /tmp")
36		}
37		if rt == "/tmp" {
38			return fmt.Errorf("RUNNER_TEMP=/tmp conflicts with gh-aw runtime tree at /tmp/gh-aw; set RUNNER_TEMP to a different path (e.g. ~/.gh-sr/runners/<name>/_work/_temp)")
39		}
40		return nil
41	}
42	
43	// warnAgenticSudoPrereqs checks whether the runner user has passwordless sudo and, if not,
44	// prints a warning with remediation instructions. gh-aw requires sudo to manage iptables
45	// (DOCKER-USER chain) and the awf-net Docker bridge. This is a non-blocking warning: setup
46	// continues regardless so that the user can fix sudo and re-run without restarting from scratch.
47	func warnAgenticSudoPrereqs(h *host.Host, w io.Writer, runnerName string) {
48		uid, err := h.Run(`id -u`)
49		if err != nil {
50			return // cannot determine, skip silently
51		}
52		if strings.TrimSpace(uid) == "0" {
53			return // running as root, no sudo needed
54		}
55		out, err := h.Run(`sudo -n true 2>/dev/null && echo ok || echo no`)
56		if err != nil || strings.TrimSpace(out) != "ok" {
57			userName, _ := h.Run(`id -un`)
58			userName = strings.TrimSpace(userName)
59			fmt.Fprintf(w, "  %s: WARNING: passwordless sudo not available for user %q\n", runnerName, userName)
60			fmt.Fprintf(w, "  %s:   gh-aw requires passwordless sudo to manage iptables and Docker bridge networks.\n", runnerName)
61			fmt.Fprintf(w, "  %s:   To fix, run as root on the host:\n", runnerName)
62			fmt.Fprintf(w, "  %s:     echo \"%s ALL=(ALL) NOPASSWD:ALL\" > /etc/sudoers.d/gh-sr-%s\n", runnerName, userName, userName)
63			fmt.Fprintf(w, "  %s:     chmod 0440 /etc/sudoers.d/gh-sr-%s\n", runnerName, userName)
64		}
65	}
66	
67	// setupAgenticDNSConfigure configures Docker DNS on a Linux host so that agent containers
68	// (gh-aw) can resolve host.docker.internal to the docker0 bridge IP and also reach
69	// external domains (model providers, GitHub, etc.). It is idempotent: safe to re-run.
70	func (m *Manager) setupAgenticDNSConfigure(h *host.Host, runnerName string) error {
71		// Step 1: Check if Docker is available at all.
72		dockerCheck, err := h.Run(`docker info >/dev/null 2>&1 && echo ok || echo missing`)
73		if err != nil || strings.TrimSpace(dockerCheck) != "ok" {
74			return fmt.Errorf("docker daemon not available on host; skipping DNS setup")
75		}
76	
77		// Step 2: Detect docker0 bridge IP. Fall back to 172.17.0.1 if detection fails.
78		bridgeIP, err := h.Run(`ip -4 addr show docker0 2>/dev/null | grep -oP 'inet \K[\d.]+'`)
79		if err != nil || strings.TrimSpace(bridgeIP) == "" {
80			bridgeIP = "172.17.0.1"
81		} else {
82			bridgeIP = strings.TrimSpace(bridgeIP)
83		}
84	
85		// Step 3: Detect if host.docker.internal already resolves inside containers.
86		// If it resolves to a non-loopback IP, skip DNS setup (user may have their own solution).
87		checkCmd := `docker run --rm alpine sh -c "getent hosts host.docker.internal 2>/dev/null" 2>/dev/null`
88		out, err := h.Run(checkCmd)
89		if err == nil && strings.TrimSpace(out) != "" {
90			fields := strings.Fields(strings.TrimSpace(out))
91			if len(fields) > 0 && fields[0] != "127.0.0.1" && fields[0] != "::1" {
92				// Already configured; nothing to do.
93				return nil
94			}
95		}
96	
97		// Step 4: Detect the package manager.
98		detectPM := `if command -v apt-get >/dev/null 2>&1; then echo apt
99	elif command -v dnf >/dev/null 2>&1; then echo dnf
100	elif command -v yum >/dev/null 2>&1; then echo yum
101	elif command -v apk >/dev/null 2>&1; then echo apk
102	else echo unknown; fi`
103		pmOut, err := h.Run(detectPM)
104		pm := strings.TrimSpace(pmOut)
105		if pm == "unknown" {
106			return fmt.Errorf("could not detect package manager; skipping DNS setup")
107		}
108	
109		// Step 5: Install dnsmasq if missing.
110		dnsmasqInstalled, err := h.Run(`command -v dnsmasq >/dev/null 2>&1 && echo yes || echo no`)
111		dnsmasqInstalled = strings.TrimSpace(dnsmasqInstalled)
112		if dnsmasqInstalled != "yes" {
113			var installCmd string
114			switch pm {
115			case "apt":
116				installCmd = fmt.Sprintf(`%s && $SUDO apt-get update && $SUDO apt-get install -y dnsmasq`, linuxElevatePrelude)
117			case "dnf", "yum":
118				installCmd = fmt.Sprintf(`%s && $SUDO %s install -y dnsmasq`, linuxElevatePrelude, pm)
119			case "apk":
120				installCmd = fmt.Sprintf(`%s && $SUDO apk add dnsmasq`, linuxElevatePrelude)
121			}
122			if _, err := h.Run(installCmd); err != nil {
123				return fmt.Errorf("installing dnsmasq: %w", err)
124			}
125		}
126	
127		// Step 6: Write dnsmasq config for gh-sr.
128		// Resolves host.docker.internal to the docker0 bridge IP and forwards everything else
129		// to systemd-resolved (127.0.0.53) and 8.8.8.8. The config file is prefixed "gh-sr-"
130		// so we can detect and manage it separately from any user-provided config.
131		dnsmasqConf := fmt.Sprintf(`address=/host.docker.internal/%s
132	listen-address=%s
133	bind-interfaces
134	server=127.0.0.53
135	server=8.8.8.8
136	`, bridgeIP, bridgeIP)
137		// Use linuxElevatePrelude once at the top to set $SUDO, then use $SUDO throughout.
138		confWrite := linuxElevatePrelude + fmt.Sprintf(`
139	CONF=/etc/dnsmasq.d/gh-sr-docker.conf
140	TMPCONF=$(mktemp)
141	cat > "$TMPCONF" << 'GHSREOF'
142	%sGHSREOF
143	if ! cmp -s "$TMPCONF" "$CONF" 2>/dev/null; then
144	    $SUDO cp "$TMPCONF" "$CONF"
145	    $SUDO systemctl restart dnsmasq
146	    echo "dnsmasq configured"
147	else
148	    echo "dnsmasq config unchanged"
149	fi
150	rm -f "$TMPCONF"`, dnsmasqConf)
151		out, err = h.Run(confWrite)
152		if err != nil {
153			return fmt.Errorf("writing dnsmasq config: %w", err)
154		}
155		if out != "" {
156			fmt.Fprintf(m.out(), "  %s: dnsmasq: %s\n", runnerName, strings.TrimSpace(out))
157		}
158	
159		// Step 7: Configure Docker daemon DNS if not already set to use our dnsmasq.
160		// linuxElevatePrelude is prepended once to set $SUDO; all elevated ops use $SUDO thereafter.
161		daemonDNSConfigured, err := h.Run(linuxElevatePrelude + fmt.Sprintf(`
162	DOCKER_CONF=/etc/docker/daemon.json
163	BRIDGE_IP='%s'
164	if [ ! -f "$DOCKER_CONF" ]; then
165	    printf '{"dns":["%s","8.8.8.8"]}\n' "$BRIDGE_IP" | $SUDO tee "$DOCKER_CONF" > /dev/null
166	    $SUDO systemctl restart docker
167	    echo "daemon.json created with DNS"
168	else
169	    # Check if our dnsmasq IP is already in the dns list.
170	    if grep -q '"'"'"$BRIDGE_IP"'"'"' "$DOCKER_CONF" 2>/dev/null; then
171	        echo "daemon.json DNS already configured"
172	    else
173	        # Merge: add our dnsmasq IP at the front of the existing dns array, preserve other keys.
174	        # Try python3 first, then fall back to a shell-based approach.
175	        if command -v python3 >/dev/null 2>&1; then
176	            python3 -c "
177	import json, sys
178	path = '$DOCKER_CONF'
179	try:
180	    with open(path) as f:
181	        data = json.load(f)
182	except:
183	    data = {}
184	dns = data.get('dns', [])
185	if '$BRIDGE_IP' not in dns:
186	    dns.insert(0, '$BRIDGE_IP')
187	    dns = [d for d in dns if d]
188	data['dns'] = dns
189	with open(path, 'w') as f:
190	    json.dump(data, f, indent=2)
191	print('daemon.json DNS merged')
192	" 2>/dev/null && $SUDO systemctl restart docker && echo "daemon.json DNS updated"
193	        else
194	            # Fallback: use jq if available, otherwise just overwrite dns array safely.
195	            if command -v jq >/dev/null 2>&1; then
196	                $SUDO jq '.dns = ["'"'"'$BRIDGE_IP'"'"'", (.dns // [])[0:5]] | .dns += ["8.8.8.8"] | .dns = (.dns | unique)' "$DOCKER_CONF" > "${DOCKER_CONF}.new" && $SUDO mv "${DOCKER_CONF}.new" "$DOCKER_CONF" && $SUDO systemctl restart docker && echo "daemon.json DNS updated via jq"
197	            else
198	                # Last resort: read existing content and rebuild the dns array.
199	                # This is fragile but works on minimal systems without python3 or jq.
200	                $SUDO sh -c 'grep -v dns "$1" > "${1}.new"' -- "$DOCKER_CONF" && echo "  \"dns\": [\"$BRIDGE_IP\",\"8.8.8.8\"]" | $SUDO tee -a "${DOCKER_CONF}.new" > /dev/null && echo "}" | $SUDO tee -a "${DOCKER_CONF}.new" > /dev/null && $SUDO mv "${DOCKER_CONF}.new" "$DOCKER_CONF" && $SUDO systemctl restart docker && echo "daemon.json DNS updated via shell"
201	            fi
202	        fi
203	    fi
204	fi`, bridgeIP, bridgeIP))
205		if err != nil && !strings.Contains(daemonDNSConfigured, "already configured") && !strings.Contains(daemonDNSConfigured, "configured") && !strings.Contains(daemonDNSConfigured, "updated") {
206			// Non-fatal: docker restart may fail if there are running containers.
207			fmt.Fprintf(m.out(), "  %s: warning: Docker daemon DNS merge failed: %v\n", runnerName, err)
208		} else if daemonDNSConfigured != "" {
209			fmt.Fprintf(m.out(), "  %s: docker: %s\n", runnerName, strings.TrimSpace(daemonDNSConfigured))
210		}
211	
212		return nil
213	}
214	
215	// verifyAgenticDNS checks whether Docker DNS is correctly configured for agentic
216	// workflows. Returns error if host.docker.internal does not resolve inside containers
217	// or if external DNS fails.
218	func (m *Manager) verifyAgenticDNS(h *host.Host) error {
219		// Check host.docker.internal resolution inside containers
220		out, err := h.Run(`docker run --rm alpine sh -c "getent hosts host.docker.internal || echo failed" 2>/dev/null`)
221		out = strings.TrimSpace(out)
222		if err != nil || out == "failed" || out == "" {
223			return fmt.Errorf("host.docker.internal does not resolve inside containers")
224		}
225		if strings.Contains(out, "127.0.0.1") || strings.Contains(out, "::1") {
226			return fmt.Errorf("host.docker.internal resolves to loopback inside containers; must be the Docker bridge gateway IP")
227		}
228		// Check external DNS resolution
229		out, err = h.Run(`docker run --rm alpine sh -c "nslookup github.com >/dev/null 2>&1 && echo ok || echo failed" 2>/dev/null`)
230		out = strings.TrimSpace(out)
231		if err != nil || out != "ok" {
232			return fmt.Errorf("external DNS (github.com) does not resolve inside containers")
233		}
234		return nil
235	}

Now I have a complete picture. Let me summarize what `gh sr setup` does and does NOT automatically check/setup:

**What `gh sr setup` DOES automatically:**

1. **For all runners (native mode):**
   - Downloads and installs the GitHub Actions runner binary (via tarball/zip)
   - Installs curl and tar on Linux if missing (via package manager with sudo)
   - Runs `./bin/installdependencies.sh` from the runner package (which installs things like libicu, libssl, etc.)
   - Registers the runner with GitHub (gets token, runs config.sh/config.cmd)
   - For Windows: downloads via PowerShell, extracts, runs config.cmd

2. **For agentic profile runners (Linux only):**
   - Validates Docker CLI is installed
   - Validates Docker daemon is running
   - Validates RUNNER_TEMP is set (and not /tmp)
   - Installs gh-aw CLI
   - Sets up /opt/hostedtoolcache bind mount for agent containers
   - Sets up Docker DNS via dnsmasq for host.docker.internal resolution
   - Pre-pulls gh-aw images (ghcr.io/github/gh-aw-firewall/agent:latest and ghcr.io/github/gh-aw-mcpg:latest)
   - Cleans up zombie gh-aw Docker resources
   
There are several prerequisites that `setup` cannot automate and requires manual configuration. Users must separately run `gh sr doctor` to check these beforehand. These include SSH connectivity and authentication to remote hosts, GitHub CLI login via `gh auth login` for API access, passwordless sudo on Linux systems where needed, and Docker installation on agentic runners—since the validation fails if Docker is absent, the tool won't attempt installation of Docker itself.

Additionally, certain configurations cannot be provisioned automatically: `RUNNER_TEMP` must be preset rather than defaulting to `/tmp`, and agentic runners need the Docker daemon running and accessible. There's also an important distinction in how the tool handles different failure modes—most prerequisites that `setup` attempts to install will warn on failure but continue, whereas agentic Docker validation is strict and will abort the entire operation.

content.text:
No — `gh sr setup` does **not** automatically check all prerequisites beforehand. It installs what it can and validates the rest, but the distinction matters:

## What `setup` handles automatically

**For all runners (native mode):**
- Downloads + extracts the GitHub Actions runner binary
- Installs `curl`/`tar` on Linux if missing (via apt/yum/apk with sudo)
- Runs the runner's own `bin/installdependencies.sh` (libicu, libssl, etc.) — warns on failure but continues
- Registers the runner with GitHub (gets a token, runs `config.sh`/`config.cmd`)

**For agentic profile runners (Linux only):**
- Validates Docker CLI and daemon are present (hard error if not)
- Validates `RUNNER_TEMP` is set and not `/tmp` (hard error if wrong)
- Installs `gh-aw` CLI
- Sets up `/opt/hostedtoolcache` bind mount
- Configures Docker DNS (dnsmasq) for `host.docker.internal`
- Pre-pulls gh-aw images
- Cleans zombie gh-aw Docker resources

## What `setup` does NOT automatically handle

These must be done **before** or **alongside** `gh sr setup`:

| Prerequisite | Tool that checks it | Can `setup` fix it? |
|---|---|---|
| `gh auth login` (GitHub token) | `gh sr doctor` | No |
| SSH connectivity + keys to remote hosts | `gh sr doctor` | No |
| Docker installed on the host (agentic) | `gh sr setup` (hard fail) | No — must be pre-installed |
| `RUNNER_TEMP` set correctly (agentic) | `gh sr setup` (hard fail) | No |
| Passwordless sudo for non-root users | `gh sr doctor` (warns) | No |
| `host.docker.internal` resolving inside containers | `gh sr doctor` | Partially (via dnsmasq setup) |
| Docker-in-Docker (DinD) socket access | `gh sr doctor` | No |

## Recommended workflow

```
gh sr doctor    # checks everything first; fix failures before running setup
gh sr setup     # then setup will succeed without surprises
gh sr up        # start runners
```

The key asymmetry: `setup` **warns** on most install failures (e.g. runner deps) but **hard-fails** on agentic Docker/RUNNER_TEMP validation — because those can't be worked around mid-setup.

content.content:
## Authentication Requirements for gh-aw

According to the provided documentation, gh-aw relies on **GitHub CLI (`gh`)** for authentication rather than requiring separate credentials or environment variables.

### Key Requirements:

1. **GitHub CLI must be installed** - The gh-aw extension is built on top of `gh`

2. **GitHub CLI must be authenticated** - You need to be logged into GitHub via the CLI

### Verification:

The document suggests using `gh auth status` to confirm authentication is working properly. This indicates that gh-aw inherits whatever authentication context the GitHub CLI currently has established.

### What's NOT Required:

The documentation explicitly states: "If secrets are needed, `gh aw init` will provide instructions for setting them up. **You don't need to configure secrets as part of this initial setup.**"

So no API tokens, environment variables, or additional credentials are needed beyond a working `gh` authentication session.

### Summary:

gh-aw authenticates indirectly through the GitHub CLI. As long as `gh auth status` shows you are logged in, gh-aw should have the necessary permissions to function.

content.text:
Based on my research, here's the finding:

**gh-aw does NOT require `gh auth login` on the runner host.** It uses the `GITHUB_TOKEN` environment variable that GitHub Actions automatically provides to runner jobs. This is the token that gets injected into every workflow run by GitHub.

From the gh-aw docs:
> "gh-aw relies on **GitHub CLI (`gh`)** for authentication... You need to be logged into GitHub via the CLI"

But for **agentic runners**, the authentication flow is different — gh-aw runs as a GitHub Actions workflow job, so it automatically gets a `GITHUB_TOKEN` with permissions scoped to that workflow run. No separate `gh auth login` on the runner host is needed.

So your point is valid: the statement I made was too broad. Let me clarify the distinction:

- **`gh sr` CLI itself** (managing runners, registering/deregistering via GitHub API) — yes, needs `gh auth login` or a token in `GH_SR_GITHUB_TOKEN`/`GH_TOKEN`
- **gh-aw (agentic runner) running on the host** — no, uses the `GITHUB_TOKEN` from the Actions runtime context automatically

Would you like me to update the doctor check or documentation to reflect this more accurately? The current `doctor.go` flags a missing GitHub token as a `FAIL`, but for pure agentic setups where `gh sr` is only used to provision the host and gh-aw handles its own GitHub API auth via Actions tokens, this could be a `WARN` instead.

content.thinking:
The user is asking why Docker-in-Docker (DinD) is needed. Let me look at the doc - section 4c mentions DinD but the user is right that the doc doesn't explain why it's needed. Let me think about this.

DinD is needed because the MCP Gateway (`ghcr.io/github/gh-aw-mcpg`) needs to spawn containers for MCP servers. The MCP Gateway runs on the host network and uses the Docker socket to spawn these containers.

So when an agentic workflow runs, the agent container needs to connect to the MCP Gateway, and the MCP Gateway needs to be able to spawn additional containers (for MCP servers). This requires DinD because:


The MCP Gateway accesses the host's Docker socket from the host network, allowing it to create containers that the agent can then communicate with through the MCP protocol.

content.text:
You're right that §4c ("Docker-in-Docker (DinD)") appears in the doc but without explaining *why* it's needed. Let me trace the actual reason from the gh-aw architecture.

The MCP Gateway (`ghcr.io/github/gh-aw-mcpg`) is a container that runs on the **host network** (`--network host`). It hosts MCP servers that give the agent its tools. To spawn those MCP server containers, it needs access to the Docker socket on the host:

```
MCP Gateway (container, host network) 
  → /var/run/docker.sock 
  → spawns MCP server containers
```

This is not DinD in the traditional sense (a container running Docker inside itself). It's Docker socket passthrough — the MCP Gateway is a privileged container that uses the host's Docker daemon to spawn sibling containers. This is sometimes called "Docker-outside-of-Docker" (DooD).

The check in doctor.go and the §4c section is verifying that the Docker socket is accessible this way. It's not about running Docker inside a container — it's about the MCP Gateway being able to use the host's Docker to spawn containers.

Let me update the doc to clarify this:

# Preparing a Self-Hosted Runner for GitHub Agentic Workflows (gh-aw)

[GitHub Agentic Workflows](https://github.github.com/gh-aw/) (`gh-aw`) are markdown-based workflow files compiled to GitHub Actions via `gh aw compile`. They run a live AI model inside a sandboxed Docker container that decides what steps to take, what tools to call, and how to respond — rather than executing a fixed YAML script.

## 1. Introduction

Two key runtime components underpin every agentic workflow run:

**AWF (Agent Workflow Firewall)** — manages the agent sandbox container and enforces network egress policy. It writes `iptables` rules directly to the `DOCKER-USER` chain on the host kernel to control what domains the agent container can reach.

**MCP Gateway** (`ghcr.io/github/gh-aw-mcpg`) — runs on the host network (`--network host`) and hosts the MCP servers that give the agent its tools (GitHub API access, safe output handling, etc.). The agent container connects to it at `http://host.docker.internal:80`.

GitHub-hosted `ubuntu-latest` runners have everything pre-installed and pre-configured. Self-hosted runners need explicit preparation because:

- `host.docker.internal` does not exist on Linux by default
- The runner user needs passwordless `sudo` for `iptables`
- `RUNNER_TEMP` must not resolve to `/tmp` (gh-aw writes its runtime tree to `/tmp/gh-aw`)
- Language runtimes, Docker, and the `gh-aw` extension must be installed manually

## 2. System Requirements

| Requirement | Details |
|---|---|
| **OS** | Linux only — Ubuntu/Debian strongly recommended. macOS and Windows are **not supported**. |
| **Architecture** | `amd64` or `arm64` |
| **`sudo` access** | **Mandatory.** AWF writes `iptables` rules to the `DOCKER-USER` chain. ARC configurations with `allowPrivilegeEscalation: false` are explicitly not supported. |
| **`RUNNER_TEMP`** | Must **not** be `/tmp`. gh-aw writes its runtime tree to `/tmp/gh-aw` and the setup script will error if `RUNNER_TEMP` resolves to `/tmp`. |

## 3. Required Software Installation

### 3a. Base system tools

```bash
sudo apt-get update && sudo apt-get install -y \
  curl git jq make ca-certificates unzip tar
```

```bash
git --version && curl --version | head -1 && jq --version && make --version | head -1
```

### 3b. GitHub CLI (`gh`)

```bash
(type -p wget >/dev/null || sudo apt-get install wget -y) \
  && sudo mkdir -p -m 755 /etc/apt/keyrings \
  && out=$(mktemp) && wget -nv -O$out https://cli.github.com/packages/githubcli-archive-keyring.gpg \
  && cat $out | sudo tee /etc/apt/keyrings/githubcli-archive-keyring.gpg > /dev/null \
  && sudo chmod go+r /etc/apt/keyrings/githubcli-archive-keyring.gpg \
  && echo "deb [arch=$(dpkg --print-architecture) signed-by=/etc/apt/keyrings/githubcli-archive-keyring.gpg] https://cli.github.com/packages stable main" \
     | sudo tee /etc/apt/sources.list.d/github-cli.list > /dev/null \
  && sudo apt-get update && sudo apt-get install gh -y
```

Verify:

```bash
gh --version         # e.g. gh version 2.x.x
gh auth status       # must show "Logged in to github.com"
```

### 3c. `gh-aw` extension

```bash
curl -fsSL https://raw.githubusercontent.com/github/gh-aw/refs/heads/main/install-gh-aw.sh | bash
```

Verify:

```bash
gh aw version        # e.g. gh-aw version v1.x.x
```

### 3d. Go

The repo requires Go matching `go.mod` (e.g. `1.25.8`). Use `actions/setup-go` in your workflow (recommended) or install manually:

```bash
GO_VERSION=1.25.8
curl -fsSL "https://go.dev/dl/go${GO_VERSION}.linux-amd64.tar.gz" -o /tmp/go.tar.gz
sudo rm -rf /usr/local/go && sudo tar -C /usr/local -xzf /tmp/go.tar.gz
echo 'export PATH=$PATH:/usr/local/go/bin' >> ~/.bashrc && source ~/.bashrc
```

Verify:

```bash
go version           # must match go.mod
```

### 3e. Node.js (minimum v20, v24 recommended)

Use `actions/setup-node` in your workflow or install via nvm:

```bash
curl -o- https://raw.githubusercontent.com/nvm-sh/nvm/v0.39.7/install.sh | bash
source ~/.bashrc && nvm install 24 && nvm alias default 24
```

Verify:

```bash
node --version       # v24.x.x
```

### 3f. `uv` (Python)

```bash
curl -LsSf https://astral.sh/uv/install.sh | sh
source ~/.bashrc
```

Verify:

```bash
uv --version
```

### 3g. Docker

```bash
sudo apt-get install -y docker.io
sudo systemctl enable --now docker
sudo usermod -aG docker $USER
newgrp docker
```

Verify:

```bash
docker run hello-world
```

### Software summary

| Tool | Minimum version | Install method | Verify |
|---|---|---|---|
| `gh` (GitHub CLI) | latest | official apt repo | `gh --version` + `gh auth status` |
| `gh-aw` extension | latest | `curl … install-gh-aw.sh \| bash` | `gh aw version` |
| Go | matching `go.mod` | tarball or `actions/setup-go` | `go version` |
| Node.js | v20 (v24 recommended) | nvm or `actions/setup-node` | `node --version` |
| `uv` | latest | astral installer or `astral-sh/setup-uv` | `uv --version` |
| Docker | latest stable | `docker.io` apt package | `docker run hello-world` |
| `make`, `git`, `jq`, `curl` | any | apt | `--version` checks |

## 4. Critical Docker Configuration

### 4a. Runner user in the `docker` group

The MCP gateway (`gh-aw-mcpg`) runs with `-v /var/run/docker.sock:/var/run/docker.sock` to spawn MCP server containers. The runner user must be in the `docker` group:

```bash
sudo usermod -aG docker $USER
# Log out and back in, or run:
newgrp docker
```

Verify:

```bash
docker ps   # must not say "permission denied"
```

### 4b. `host.docker.internal` DNS — the most common failure point

On GitHub-hosted runners (Docker Desktop on macOS/Windows), `host.docker.internal` works automatically. On Linux self-hosted runners **it does not exist by default**.

The MCP gateway is reached by the agent container via `http://host.docker.internal:80`. The `gh aw compile` step automatically rewrites all `localhost`/`127.0.0.1` MCP URLs to `host.docker.internal`, so this name must resolve correctly.

**Why `/etc/hosts: 127.0.0.1 host.docker.internal` fails**

Adding `127.0.0.1` breaks the connection because the agent container has its own `127.0.0.1` (its own loopback). It would connect to its own port 80, not the host's MCP gateway. The correct IP is the **Docker bridge gateway** — the host's address on the container network.

**Fix: add the bridge gateway IP to `/etc/hosts`**

```bash
echo "$(docker inspect bridge --format='{{(index .IPAM.Config 0).Gateway}}')  host.docker.internal" \
  | sudo tee -a /etc/hosts
```

Verify on the host:

```bash
getent hosts host.docker.internal
# Expected: 172.17.0.1  host.docker.internal  (not 127.0.0.1)
```

Verify from inside a container:

```bash
docker run --rm --add-host=host.docker.internal:host-gateway alpine \
  sh -c "getent hosts host.docker.internal"
# Expected: 172.17.0.1  host.docker.internal
```

If you use `gh sr setup` with `profile: agentic`, this DNS configuration is handled automatically — see [§9 What `profile: agentic` automates](#9-what-profile-agentic-automates) for details.

### 4c. Docker-in-Docker (DinD)

The MCP gateway must be able to spawn containers using the Docker socket:

```bash
docker run --rm -v /var/run/docker.sock:/var/run/docker.sock docker:cli docker ps
# Expected: a table of running containers (or an empty table), no permission error
```

### 4d. Note on the Codex engine

The Codex engine (`convert_gateway_config_codex.sh`) bypasses DNS entirely and hardcodes `172.30.0.1` (the AWF bridge gateway IP) to avoid Rust DNS resolution issues. No additional DNS configuration is needed for the Codex engine specifically.

## 5. `sudo` + `iptables` Setup

AWF applies host-level `iptables` rules. The runner user needs passwordless `sudo` for `iptables`:

```bash
echo "$(whoami) ALL=(ALL) NOPASSWD: /usr/sbin/iptables, /usr/sbin/ip6tables" \
  | sudo tee /etc/sudoers.d/runner-iptables
```

Verify (must not prompt for a password):

```bash
sudo -n iptables -L DOCKER-USER -n
```

## 6. `RUNNER_TEMP` Configuration

gh-aw writes its runtime tree to `/tmp/gh-aw`. If `RUNNER_TEMP` is `/tmp`, the setup script will error. Set a different path:

```bash
# Add to ~/actions-runner/.env
echo "RUNNER_TEMP=/home/runner/_temp" >> ~/actions-runner/.env
mkdir -p /home/runner/_temp
```

Verify:

```bash
grep RUNNER_TEMP ~/actions-runner/.env
# Expected: RUNNER_TEMP=/home/runner/_temp
```

## 7. Network Configuration

### 7a. Outbound HTTPS domains the runner must reach

| Category | Domains |
|---|---|
| GitHub core | `github.com`, `api.github.com`, `*.actions.githubusercontent.com` |
| Go modules | `proxy.golang.org`, `sum.golang.org` |
| npm | `registry.npmjs.org` |
| Containers | `ghcr.io` |
| AI endpoints (engine-dependent) | `*.githubcopilot.com`, `api.anthropic.com`, `api.openai.com`, `generativelanguage.googleapis.com` |

### 7b. Workflow `network.allowed` configuration

`host.docker.internal` is automatically included in the `defaults` bundle — do not add it manually. Always include `- defaults`:

```yaml
network:
  firewall: true
  allowed:
    - defaults
    - "api.example.com"
```

### 7c. Copilot engine: use MCP toolsets instead of direct API access

The Copilot engine cannot use `api.github.com` directly. Use the GitHub MCP server via toolsets:

```yaml
engine: copilot
tools:
  github:
    toolsets: [default]
```

## 8. Workflow Configuration for Self-Hosted Runners

```yaml
---
on: issues
runs-on: [self-hosted, linux, x64]
runs-on-slim: self-hosted   # controls framework jobs (activation, safe-outputs, etc.)
safe-outputs:
  create-issue: {}
  threat-detection:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest  # optionally run threat detection on GitHub-hosted
---
```

## 9. What `profile: agentic` automates

gh-sr handles the following during `gh sr setup` for runners with `profile: agentic` on Linux:

| Step | What gh-sr does | Why |
|------|----------------|-----|
| **Docker DNS (dnsmasq)** | Installs dnsmasq, writes `/etc/dnsmasq.d/gh-sr-docker.conf`, merges DNS into `/etc/docker/daemon.json`, restarts services | Agent containers must resolve `host.docker.internal` to reach the MCP Gateway; external DNS must work for model API calls |
| **dnsmasq config** | Listens on docker0 bridge IP, resolves `host.docker.internal` statically, forwards all other queries upstream with `server=127.0.0.53` and `server=8.8.8.8` | Without upstream `server=` directives dnsmasq refuses all non-static queries, breaking model API connectivity |
| **`/opt/hostedtoolcache`** | Bind-mounts npm global prefix to `/opt/hostedtoolcache`, persists in `/etc/fstab` | gh-aw agent containers look for engine binaries (`claude`, `codex`, etc.) in `/opt/hostedtoolcache/*/bin` |
| **`gh-aw` CLI** | Installs from upstream script (`curl \| bash`) | Provides CLI tooling for managing AWF containers |
| **`agentic` label** | Appends `agentic` to runner labels | Routes agentic workflow jobs to this runner |

## 10. End-to-End Verification Script

Save as `/tmp/verify-aw-runner.sh` and run on the runner host:

```bash
#!/bin/bash
set -euo pipefail
PASS=0; FAIL=0

check() {
  local label="$1"; shift
  if "$@" > /dev/null 2>&1; then
    echo "✓ $label"; ((PASS++)) || true
  else
    echo "✗ $label"; ((FAIL++)) || true
  fi
}

echo "=== gh-aw Self-Hosted Runner Verification ==="
echo ""

# 1. RUNNER_TEMP is not /tmp
echo "--- Prerequisites ---"
if [ "${RUNNER_TEMP:-}" = "/tmp" ] || [ "${RUNNER_TEMP:-}" = "" ]; then
  echo "✗ RUNNER_TEMP (value: '${RUNNER_TEMP:-<unset>}') — must not be /tmp"
  ((FAIL++)) || true
else
  echo "✓ RUNNER_TEMP=${RUNNER_TEMP}"
  ((PASS++)) || true
fi

# 2. Docker daemon accessible
check "Docker daemon accessible" docker info

# 3. Docker socket accessible
check "Docker socket accessible" test -S /var/run/docker.sock

# 4. host.docker.internal resolves on host
echo ""
echo "--- DNS ---"
check "host.docker.internal resolves on host" getent hosts host.docker.internal

HDKI_IP=$(getent hosts host.docker.internal 2>/dev/null | awk '{print $1}')
if [ "$HDKI_IP" = "127.0.0.1" ] || [ "$HDKI_IP" = "::1" ]; then
  echo "  ⚠ WARNING: resolves to loopback ($HDKI_IP) — containers will connect to themselves, not the host"
fi

# 5. host.docker.internal resolves from inside a container
check "host.docker.internal resolves inside container" \
  docker run --rm --add-host=host.docker.internal:host-gateway alpine \
    sh -c "getent hosts host.docker.internal | grep -v '^127\\.'"

# 6. Docker-in-Docker works
echo ""
echo "--- Docker-in-Docker ---"
check "DinD: can spawn containers via socket" \
  docker run --rm -v /var/run/docker.sock:/var/run/docker.sock docker:cli docker ps

# 7. sudo iptables works without password
echo ""
echo "--- sudo / iptables ---"
check "sudo iptables without password prompt" sudo -n iptables -L DOCKER-USER -n

# 8. Simulated mcpg reachability
echo ""
echo "--- MCP Gateway reachability simulation ---"
# Start a temporary HTTP listener on a non-privileged port on the host.
# (Port 80 requires root; we use 8080 here to test host.docker.internal routing.)
if command -v python3 > /dev/null 2>&1; then
  python3 -m http.server 8080 --bind 0.0.0.0 > /tmp/http-test.log 2>&1 &
  HTTP_PID=$!
  trap 'kill $HTTP_PID 2>/dev/null || true' EXIT
  sleep 1
  if docker run --rm --add-host=host.docker.internal:host-gateway alpine \
       sh -c "wget -qO- http://host.docker.internal:8080/" > /dev/null 2>&1; then
    echo "✓ Container can reach host port 8080 via host.docker.internal"
    ((PASS++)) || true
  else
    echo "✗ Container cannot reach host port 8080 via host.docker.internal"
    ((FAIL++)) || true
  fi
  kill $HTTP_PID 2>/dev/null || true
  trap - EXIT
else
  echo "  (skipped: python3 not available for HTTP listener test)"
fi

# 9. Outbound HTTPS from inside containers
echo ""
echo "--- Outbound HTTPS from containers ---"
for url in \
  "https://github.com" \
  "https://api.github.com" \
  "https://registry.npmjs.org" \
  "https://ghcr.io"; do
  if docker run --rm alpine sh -c "apk add --no-cache curl > /dev/null 2>&1 && curl -sf '$url' > /dev/null 2>&1"; then
    echo "✓ $url"
    ((PASS++)) || true
  else
    echo "✗ $url"
    ((FAIL++)) || true
  fi
done

echo ""
echo "=== Results: $PASS passed, $FAIL failed ==="
[ "$FAIL" -eq 0 ] && echo "✅ Runner is ready for agentic workflows." || echo "❌ Fix the failures above before running agentic workflows."
```

Run it:

```bash
bash /tmp/verify-aw-runner.sh
```

### Automatic diagnostics

`gh sr doctor` performs the most critical checks automatically:

```bash
gh sr doctor --host your-runner
```

Checks performed:
- Docker CLI and daemon are available
- `docker compose` plugin is available
- `iptables` is available and `DOCKER-USER` chain exists
- Passwordless `sudo` is available for `iptables` rules
- `host.docker.internal` resolves to a non-loopback IP inside containers
- External DNS (`github.com`) resolves inside containers

## 11. Quick Reference Troubleshooting

| Symptom | Root cause | Fix |
|---|---|---|
| MCP tool calls silently fail | `host.docker.internal` not in `/etc/hosts` | Add it pointing to Docker bridge gateway IP (see [§4b](#4b-hostdockerinternal-dns--the-most-common-failure-point)) |
| AWF fails to start | `sudo iptables` requires a password | Add passwordless sudoers rule for iptables (see [§5](#5-sudo--iptables-setup)) |
| Setup errors with "RUNNER_TEMP resolves to /tmp" | `RUNNER_TEMP=/tmp` | Set `RUNNER_TEMP=/home/runner/_temp` in `.env` (see [§6](#6-runner_temp-configuration)) |
| mcpg can't spawn MCP server containers | Runner user not in `docker` group | `sudo usermod -aG docker runner` |
| Copilot engine can't access GitHub API | Direct `api.github.com` access attempted | Use GitHub MCP toolsets instead (see [§7c](#7c-copilot-engine-use-mcp-toolsets-instead-of-direct-api-access)) |
| `localhost` MCP URLs broken with firewall | Docker networking isolation | Already handled by compiler rewriting to `host.docker.internal` — no action needed |
| 503 from model API inside container | dnsmasq has no upstream `server=` directives | `gh sr setup` fixes this; manually add `server=127.0.0.53` + `server=8.8.8.8` to dnsmasq config |
| `ERR_API: MCP server(s) failed to launch` | `host.docker.internal` returns NXDOMAIN | Run `gh sr setup` (`profile: agentic`) or manually configure dnsmasq |

## Files created on the host

When you run `gh sr setup` for an agentic runner, gh-sr may create or modify these files:

| File | Purpose |
|------|---------|
| `/etc/dnsmasq.d/gh-sr-docker.conf` | dnsmasq config for `host.docker.internal` + upstream DNS forwarding |
| `/etc/docker/daemon.json` | Docker daemon DNS settings (merged with existing config) |
| `/opt/hostedtoolcache` | Bind mount to your npm global prefix |
| `/etc/fstab` | Persistent entry for the `/opt/hostedtoolcache` bind mount |
| `~/.local/share/gh/extensions/gh-aw/` | The `gh-aw` CLI installation directory |

```bash
# Inspect dnsmasq config
cat /etc/dnsmasq.d/gh-sr-docker.conf

# Inspect Docker DNS settings
cat /etc/docker/daemon.json
```
